EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-vehicle crash occurred in dense fog in Lafayette county around 6:45 am. There were 30 injuries and 250 gallons of diesel fuel spilled.  The crash occurred at the intersection of SR-51 and CR-354.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper-level trough lingered over the FL peninsula while the low-level trough axis was inland across NE FL.  NE flow on the 'west' side of the trough axis was steering numerous showers with embedded thunderstorms to the SW across inland NE FL, some producing training locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Hog Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous severe thunderstorms developed across east-central Florida along the east coast sea breeze, and along the main sea breeze collision. Many storms became severe over Seminole and Volusia Counties where hail and wind damage occurred. Then, as storms moved offshore they produced severe wind gusts along the coast in Brevard, Indian River, and Martin Counties. One storm produced an EF-0 tornado in Saint Lucie County west of Fort Pierce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weatherflow site XPAR at Parrish Park in Titusville measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph from the north-northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 68-year-old woman from Cocoa Beach was caught in a rip current at around 8 PM on Saturday, June 18th in New Smyrna Beach at an unguarded area of the beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media and Volusia County Ocean Rescue reported a 68-year-old woman from Cocoa Beach was caught in a rip current at around 8 PM on Saturday, June 18th in New Smyrna Beach. The incident occurred at an unguarded area of the beach. An off-duty officer noticed the woman struggling in the water, brought her onshore, and performed CPR. The woman was transported to a local hospital where she later died.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions during the early to mid afternoon on June 24, 2022 resulted in peak heat indices of 113 to 117 across Lake, Volusia, Orange, Seminole, Osceola, Okeechobee, and Brevard Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at the Okeechobee County Airport, KOBE, measured a peak heat index of 115F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed along the east coast sea breeze and other outflow boundary collisions. One such collision occurred over Saint Lucie County that rapidly led to the development of a severe thunderstorm over Port Saint Lucie. This storm produced wind damage and an EF-0 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports and videos received from the public showed downburst wind damage occurred along SE Galleon Lane, SE Lamon Lane, and SE Calmoso Lane in Port Saint Lucie. The damage consisted of downed fences, numerous small to medium sized tree branches downed, and many backyard items tossed into adjacent areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 57-year-old woman was injured after being caught in a rip current at Daytona Beach Shores. Beach safety officials pulled the woman from the surf, performed life-saving measures, and she was transported to a local hospital in stable condition.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County Ocean Rescue reported that a 57-year-old woman was injured after being caught in a rip current at Daytona Beach Shores at approximately 1240LST. The woman was caught in the rip current while trying to get back to shore and became submerged under the water. Beach safety officials rescued the unresponsive woman from the surf, and performed life-saving measures. The woman was transported to a local hospital in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in an area of convergence near the far upper Florida Keys as outflow from showers and thunderstorms over mainland south Florida moved southward. A waterspout developed and was observed making landfall along the oceanside of a community within Key Largo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-supercell-spawned waterspout was observed to have made landfall on the Atlantic side of Key Largo in the Silver Shores Community.  A spray ring was visible with the waterspout prior to landfall.  Photos revealed damage to a dock railing and a snapped mature coconut palm tree, but no damage to nearby homes was observed.  Based on all available information, the tornado was rated EF-0 with maximum winds of 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low surface low pressure formed along a frontal boundary that was draped across the local area. Aloft, shortwave impulses traversed with the better dynamic |forcing over southeast GA. The entire airmass was primed (PWAT over 2 inches) and prevailing westerly steering flow focused the strongest convection along the sea breezes mergers toward the I-95 corridor in the afternoon and into the evening. A cluster of storms formed along the front across NE FL and Duval county. When these slow moving storms encountered the east coast sea breeze as it moved inland, very heavy rainfall rates produced local flooding across portions of downtown Jacksonville, including the Hogans Creek basin near UF Shands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down from thunderstorm winds just south of Ponte Vedra Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Nicole formed as a subtropical storm about 600 miles east of the Bahamas on November 7th, then moved northwest. Nicole gained tropical characteristics and became a tropical storm on November 8th as it turned west and west-southwest. Nicole strengthened into a Category 1 hurricane on November 9th as it moved over Great Abaco and Grand Bahama islands. Nicole then turned to the northwest as it approached Florida, making landfall just south of Vero Beach at 3 AM on November 10th as a Category 1 hurricane with lowest central pressure of 981 mb. Nicole then was downgraded to a tropical storm while moving inland across Central Florida.||Nicole's large wind field west of the storm center led to tropical storm force winds along the SE Florida coast and adjacent Atlantic waters beginning on the afternoon of November 8th and continuing until the pre-dawn hours of November 10th. Tropical storm force winds also spread across Lake Okeechobee during the late night of November 9th and early morning of November 10th. Peak winds over land areas were in the 50-60 mph range, confined to the immediate coastal areas.||Storm tide from Nicole was measured at 2.52 ft above Mean Higher High Water (MHHW) at Lake Worth Pier, 2.09 ft above MHHW at South Port Everglades, and 2.14 feet above MHHW at Virginia Key. Tidal flooding and beach erosion were observed along most of the SE Florida coast from the Miami area northward through the Palm Beaches. ||Storm total rainfall was in the 3-5 inch range across SE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest sustained winds were in the 40-55 mph range across coastal sections of Palm Beach County, with a peak measured sustained wind of 54 mph at Juno Beach Pier at 8:53 PM ET on November 9th, with a corresponding peak wind gust of 66 mph. ||Wind damage was minor, and a total of 51,680 customers lost power across all of Palm Beach County.

Rough surf caused significant damage to the the piers at Hillsboro Beach and Lauderdale-by-the-Sea. Moderate coastal flooding was observed in the typically vulnerable areas on the barrier islands, as well as on the mainland side of the Intracoastal Waterway. State road A1a in Fort Lauderdale Beach was closed due to persistent flooding, with waters water encroaching on structures on the west side of the highway. The ocean washed over the boardwalk in Hollywood and came up to the front door of structures on the beachfront. Many parts of the barrier island in Pompano Beach were flooded with several inches of water, and water entered at least one garage. Flooded streets were also reported on the mainland side of the Intracoastal Waterway in Fort Lauderdale.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout moved onshore the afternoon of the 30th, tossing and|destroying a large shed based on numerous eyewitness reports and|video. The tornado was rated EF0 with winds up to 85mph. The|tornado dissipated quickly before reaching Highway 98.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal trough over the local Atlantic waters approached the local Atlantic coast during the pre-dawn hours which shifted bands of heavy rainfall onshore. Convergence along the trough axis and lift from central FL ahead of an approaching warm front combined with high tropical moisture (PWATs 1.7 to 2 inches) under SW flow aloft produced localized heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mayport AWOS KNRB measured 2.67 inches of rainfall between 4:20 am and 5:10 am.

The onshore gradient between these two features produced gusty winds and enhanced low level isentropic lift near the meandering front which produced periods of heavy rainfall across portions of the coast and NE FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station measured 3.71 inches of rainfall in 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the area during the overnight hours of October 25th.  A marginal risk of severe storms was expected from the Storm Prediction Center due to a low end combination of instability and wind shear.  One brief tornado occurred in Holmes county, resulting in minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in a densely wooded area west of Henry Grey road in southeastern Holmes county around 1114 pm CDT. The starting point was based on a tornadic debris signature (TDS) from the Eglin Air Force Base radar, as access to this area is extremely limited. The tornado continued east just north of Sand Path Road, but was likely quite weak by the time it reached Henry Grey road. No damage was noted to homes near this intersection. The only damage noted along the path was along Barefield Lane. At this location, a small metal outbuilding was lifted and tossed into a nearby power pole. In addition, numerous fence panels were blown out. Finally, a large tree limb fell onto a vehicle. However, no structural damage to any of the surrounding manufactured homes was noted. Maximum winds were estimated at this location to be around 75 mph. The tornado likely dissipated at this point at approximately 1119 pm CDT. No additional damage was seen to the east of this location, and the TDS dissipated on radar. The tornado was rated an EF0 with max winds around 75 mph. Thanks to Holmes County Emergency management for aiding in this assessment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the region during the early morning hours of January 16th. Limited instability kept thunderstorms confined to near the coast. However, a few reports of severe weather did occur along the Florida big bend with one tornado confirmed on Dog Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved across portions of Dog Island early Sunday morning with damage to one residence reported. The roof was partially torn off with significant damage to the porch. This damage was consistent with an EF1 tornado with winds estimated around 95 mph. In addition, damage to trees and power lines occurred with Duke Energy reporting 97 customers without power on the island. A tornadic debris signature (TDS) was evident on TLH radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports received from the public of ping pong to golf ball sized hail along State Road 70 near the Saint Lucie Fairgrounds. This hail resulted in significant damage to many vehicles in the area, including broken wind shields, windows, and mirrors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and line of storms moved across central and southwest Florida during the early morning producing multiple tornadoes and strong straight line wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Placida/Gasparilla tornado. A waterspout moved across Gasparilla Sound near Boca Grande Causeway before moving ashore near Placida as a short-lived EF-1 tornado with peak winds estimated around 110 mph. The tornado flipped a boat at a marina storage facility and damaged several others before moving into a manufactured home community where it damaged at least 35 homes. No injuries or deaths were reported. Damage estimates totaled around $500 thousand USD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and line of storms moved across central and southwest Florida during the early morning producing multiple tornadoes and strong straight line wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Iona/Fort Myers tornado. A waterspout first identified just northwest of Fort Myers Beach moved ashore as a tornado and pushed through the Iona area near Fort Myers producing EF-2 damage with peak winds estimated around 118 mph before dissipating near the Cape Coral bridge. Over 200 residences suffered damage across three separate mobile home communities (Point Breeze, Tropicana, and Century 21), with 45 destroyed, 90 sustaining major damage and another 92 with minor damage. 3 people suffered minor injuries. Damage estimates totaled around $10 million USD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and line of storms moved across central and southwest Florida during the early morning producing multiple tornadoes and strong straight line wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Port Charlotte tornado. Tornado briefly touched down in the Village of Holiday Lake near Port Charlotte with peak winds estimated around 100 mph. This tornado was produced by the parent thunderstorm that earlier produced the Placida/Gasparilla tornado. Four homes were deemed unsafe/destroyed; two with major damage, and two with minor damage. Roof and carports had major damage or were destroyed. No injuries or deaths reported. Damage estimates totaled around $150 thousand USD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and line of storms moved across central and southwest Florida during the early morning producing multiple tornadoes and strong straight line wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cape Coral tornado. Brief tornado near Cape Coral was captured on a home doorbell camera. No significant damage was reported and the tornado was rated as an EF-0 with peak winds estimated around 65 mph. No injuries or deaths reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a front across South Florida along with cold air aloft led to high amounts of instability which fostered the development of strong thunderstorms over interior portions of South Florida. Large hail up to 2 inches in diameter was the main impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Everglades National Park employees reported penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strengthening easterly flow contributed to building seas, northeasterly swell, and rough surf across the Atlantic waters. This led to hazardous marine and beach conditions to the South Florida Atlantic coast beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that 3 swimmers were transported to the hospital after becoming caught in a rip current near the 800 block of South Seabreeze Boulevard/A1A on Fort Lauderdale Beach at around 6:20 PM. One swimmer, a 21-year-old male, went into cardiac arrest at the hospital and passed away 4 days later, while the other two were released from the hospital the same day.||Lifeguards had just left their stations for the day about 30 minutes prior to the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions over interior South Florida led to the development of severe drought conditions over western Palm Beach County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions over the past 4 months led to severe drought conditions over western Palm Beach County. Rainfall across that area was 4 inches or less since December 1st, which was about 10-25% of normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions led to the development of severe drought in eastern Glades County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low rainfall amounts since December 1st led to the development of severe drought conditions across eastern Glades County. Rainfall estimates in these areas were only in the 10-25% of normal range for that time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions over SW Florida led to the development of severe drought conditions over the western half of Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lack of significant rainfall over the previous 4 months led to the development of severe drought (D2) conditions over the western half Collier County. Only 1.62 of rain was measured in Marco Island and 2.65 at Naples Airport from December 1st through March 31st. This was approximately 10 to 25% of the normal value for that time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged dry conditions over SW Florida led to the development of severe drought conditions over the western half of Collier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lack of significant rainfall over the previous 4 months led to the development of severe drought (D2) conditions over the western half Collier County. Collier County implemented a burn ban.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist, light south winds contributed to areas of dense fog during the pre-dawn hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two car accidents occurred within 15 minutes of each other in inland Flagler county during the predawn hours, likely due to poor visibility from fog in the area. A car accident near the intersection of State Road 100 and County Road 305 resulted in 1 fatality. Another car accident occurred around 6 am at State Road 100 and Water Oak Road which caused 2 fatalities. Dense fog was a possible contributor to both crashes per local News Reports, however, at this time, both accidents remain under investigation by Law Enforcement. The Flagler County Sheriff closed State Road 100 from near County Road 305 westward to the Putnam County line that morning after the crashes due to foggy conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the SE United States dragged its associated cold front across the FL peninsula, bringing severe thunderstorms to the region. A band of thunderstorms moved through the area, with strong winds aloft providing the necessary ingredients for a few waterspouts/tornadoes as well as strong and gusty straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported via social media a downed tree and powerlines on Buchanan Street in Hollywood due to strong thunderstorm wind gusts. Estimated wind speeds in the 55 to 65 mph range.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 78 mph was measured at the WeatherSTEM station at the University of Miami RSMAS campus in Virginia Key. The anemometer height is elevated on top of a building, therefore winds closer to the surface were likely lower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unsettled weather pattern set up across South FL as a plume of tropical moisture settled across the region. The excess moisture along with the presence of a surface boundary along the coast promoted heavy rainfall and flooding across portions of Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public shared via social media videos of flash flooding in downtown Fort Lauderdale showing water above curbs, over sidewalks, and entering ground floor store fronts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through South Florida late on January 29th, ushering in cold air of Arctic origin into the region. Two nights of freezing temperatures followed across interior South Florida, with the lowest temperatures occurring on the morning of January 30th. Temperatures in the mid to upper 20s were recorded in Glades, Hendry, and interior Collier counties (the lowest in 10-11 years in some areas) and 30s over most of the remainder of South Florida with the exception of the urban corridor of Miami and Fort Lauderdale. Wind chill readings dropped into the mid 20s to lower 30s over most of South Florida on the morning of January 30th.||The front was also accompanied by strong winds of 40-50 mph which affected much of SE Florida during the early morning of January 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 4-8 hours of freezing temperatures were registered in Glades County on the morning of January 30th. The lowest recorded temperature was 25 degrees at the Florida Automated Weather Network (FAWN) site in Palmdale. Duration of hard freeze (28 degrees or lower) was approximately 1 to 3 hours. No details on crop damage, but overall damage was noted to pastureland and sensitive fruits and vegetables.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms ahead of a cold front brought severe weather to the tri-state area.  Several reports of trees and power lines blown down were received from across the region along with one tornado in Walton county, FL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down just north of the De Funiak Springs Municipal Airport near the intersection of Dr Roberts Drive and Bellini Road. The tornado uprooted a few trees and caused damage to several outbuildings and two mobile homes. The maximum wind is estimated at 80 mph, making this an EF-0 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front in the vicinity of South FL and a series of mid-level impulses promoted instability and thus convection over the nearshore Atlantic waters off the Miami-Dade County coast and right along the coast due to coastal convergence. This combined with lingering moisture and slow storm motion allowed for heavy rainfall and flooding to occur across portions of Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS meteorologist reported via social media and a phone call that flooding was ongoing at the intersection of SE 9th Avenue and SE 1st Street in downtown Miami. Video footage shows several inches of standing water on the roadway/curb as well as water entering the first floor of buildings. Water was halfway up car tires as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the SE United States dragged its associated cold front across the FL peninsula, bringing severe thunderstorms to the region. A band of thunderstorms moved through the area, with strong winds aloft providing the necessary ingredients for a few waterspouts/tornadoes as well as strong and gusty straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video from the public at Isle of Capri shared via social media captured the tornado as a waterspout before it came ashore. Surveyed tornado damage was found in the Victoria Falls neighborhood west of Collier Blvd and south of Tamiami Trail. A total of 20 homes sustained at least minor damage. Several uprooted trees, broken large branches, and at least two homes with tarps on roofs indicative of roof damage were observed in this neighborhood. ||Tornado damage was also observed north of Tamiami Trail in Lely Resort. Here, the surveyed damage was mainly in the form of a few uprooted small trees, one uprooted palm tree, and broken large tree branches. The tops of a few palm trees were partially sheared off at the crown with only a few fronds remaining as well. A street sign was knocked over on Palmer Ct in Lely Resort. ||As the cell moved to the northeast, the tornado eventually crossed Interstate 75 near mile marker 96 where it blew over a semi-tractor trailer, injuring the driver, according to the Florida Highway Patrol. Estimated peak wind of 85 mph - high-end EF-0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values were only around an inch with dry mid levels. Moisture slowly increased in the afternoon with sea breeze initiated strong to pulse severe storms in the late afternoon and evening along mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gainesville ASOS measured a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values were only around an inch with dry mid levels. Moisture slowly increased in the afternoon with sea breeze initiated strong to pulse severe storms in the late afternoon and evening along mergers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low along the coast of southeast Georgia moved slowly inland through the day. The 12Z JAX sounding showed steep low/mid lapse rates and cool temperatures aloft. MUCAPE was also over 3000 J/kg. Winds throughout the column were relatively light , and sea breeze boundaries moved inland along both coasts. Scattered thunderstorm developed along the sea breeze boundaries with a merger near the I-75 corridor in the late afternoon where a few strong to severe storms formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The FL Highway Patrol reported a tree blown down across Highway 129 near 254th Terrace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous afternoon thunderstorms developed across east-central Florida as the east coast sea breeze moved inland from the coast, and eventually collided with the west coast sea breeze. Many storms became severe and produced quarter to half dollar sized hail over parts of Seminole, Volusia, and Lake Counties. Another severe thunderstorm in Lake County produced straight-line wind damage in Tavares.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter-sized hail in Tavares.

A home in the Lake Dora Manor subdivision sustained minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across northwest Florida and produced large hail.

Strong to severe thunderstorms formed along a prefrontal trough in the evening which was driven by a potent mid level shortwave trough that slowly rotated across the Deep South through the following day.  At the surface, a cyclone deepened as it tracked into the Tennessee/Ohio valleys during the evening and it resulted in a strengthening |low level wind field including a strong LLJ (around 50 kts) that lifted across the interior portions of SE GA with the core of  the jet skirting just northwest of SE GA. As the LLJ |strengthened, an ongoing quasi-linear complex of storms pushed into SE GA along a SW-NE oriented prefrontal trough. Instability (1500-2000 j/kg) and shear (150-200 m2/s2) supported of all modes of severe weather (damaging wind, hail, and isolated tornadoes) as activity propagated into SE GA and portions of NE FL into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was struck by lightning north of Macclenny. The person was alert when medics arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms organized to the west of the area during the early morning hours and raced across the area during the morning. The storms produced several reports of wind damage along with two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees in the Riverview area along Scenic Highway.

As the LLJ |strengthened, an ongoing quasi-linear complex of storms pushed into SE GA along a SW-NE oriented prefrontal trough. Instability (1500-2000 j/kg) and shear (150-200 m2/s2) supported of all modes of severe weather (damaging wind, hail, and isolated tornadoes) as activity propagated into SE GA and portions of NE FL into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and blocked the roadway. The time of the damage was based on radar. The cost of damage was estimated for inclusion of the event in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense line of storms produced wind damage in and around the Pensacola metro area along with a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey team found scattered locations of wind damage across the |Pensacola/Ferry Pass areas in and around the vicinity of North Davis|Dr. For much of this damage, a clear convergent track could not be|identified and appears to be the result of straight line winds of 60 to |80 mph. Pensacola International measured a thunderstorm wind gust|of 59 mph as the line of storms moved through. ||Winds appeared to strengthen near North Davis Dr to the south of I-10|and this helped to generate a brief tornado in the E Olive Rd/Whitmire Dr|areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding in Santa Rosa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding was reported on Pearson Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak pressure pattern was across the local area with a ridge across the western Atlantic and a mid/upper level across the Carolinas to Florida panhandle with weak SW flow aloft over the local area. Mid level temps warmed, but mid level lapse rates were modest around 6.7 degC/km with surface based CAPE increasing to near 2500 J/kg in the afternoon with surface dew pts near 70 degF. Shear was not impressive, but neared 15-25 kts across NE FL in the afternoon which enabled some stronger cells to form with gusty wind, hail and weak tornado potential.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed on social media. Report shared by Broadcast Meteorologist.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the western Atlantic led to a steady onshore wind flow across the Atlantic coast of South Florida, creating ideal conditions for rip current formation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several people were pulled out of the water and a 47-year-old male from Palm Beach County died after getting caught in a rip current at Midtown Beach in the Town of Palm Beach. Six additional people were transported to a local hospital with non-life-threatening injuries, including a police officer who assisted with the rescues. Five of the 6 rescued were from out of state. Lifeguards were off duty during the time of the incident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video was received of a funnel cloud over Wiggins Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported by the public in Wellington. Report received via social media.

The incident occurred along the 16500 block of Wilson Parrish Road in Umatilla at approximately 1910LST. The disoriented man was transported to a local hospital in Ocala, and was released later that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid/upper level low over the eastern Gulf of Mexico continued to feed moisture and instability into South Florida, helping to trigger strong thunderstorms over parts of SE Florida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge was anchored to the east in the western Atlantic while a ridge aloft was building northward up the Florida peninsula. A weak surface circulation developed to the|northwest overnight and shifted NNEward through western Alabama as a trough axis (pseudo cold front) appended to this low shifted eastward into southeast GA and the Suwannee Valley during the late morning and afternoon hours. This trough axis over a moist and diurnally unstable airmass fueled a few strong to severe storms. Strong diurnal instability and a pocket of dry air in the low levels enhanced downburst potential. PWATs were around the 75th percentile bringing a threat of locally heavy rain and potential for localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 9 trees were blown down along U.S. Highway 41 between White Springs and Suwannee Springs. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values were only around an inch with dry mid levels. Moisture slowly increased in the afternoon with sea breeze initiated strong to pulse severe storms in the late afternoon and evening along mergers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near mile marker 378 along Interstate 75. The time of the event was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions since December 2021 persisted into the beginning of May across Collier County. Periods of heavy rainfall during the first week of May led to improving conditions by the second week of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much below normal rainfall persisted into early May across inland sections of Collier County, leading to the continuation of severe drought (D2) conditions which began in April. Periods of heavy rainfall during the first week of May led to gradually improving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across the area during the pre-dawn hours and shifted off of the local Atlantic coast during the morning, which produced an early morning tornado in Nassau County. Another wave of strong to isolated severe storms impacted the local area in the afternoon with convection that formed along the actual cold front. Non-convective gradient wind gusts were 30 to 40 mph outside of thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the parking lot at Crossroads Plaza was flooded. Water was flowing over the curbs. A video was shared via Social Media from a broadcast partner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stationary boundary across the I-10 corridor lifted northward as a warm front on Easter day. Behind the front, substantial surface heating, cold temperatures aloft, and an unstable air mass resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms developing along the inland moving sea breezes for portions of northeast FL in the afternoon.  Severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to 2 inches in the vicinity of Nocatee and Palm Valley in northern St. Johns County and Palm Coast in Flagler County along the active Atlantic sea breeze boundary. A severe thunderstorm also downed several trees in Ocala due to a mircoburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail was reported by the public near Guana River State Park at 1:40 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection moved inland from the Gulf of Mexico through midday ahead of a deepening upper level trough. Elevated thunderstorms along and north of a lifting warm front produced hail across NE FL with strong westerly flow from 500-300 mb of 65-75 kts. There were widespread reports of pea to penny size hail across NE FL with storms (0.25 to 0.75 inches in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along County Road 234. The time of the damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stationary boundary across the I-10 corridor lifted northward as a warm front on Easter day. Behind the front, substantial surface heating, cold temperatures aloft, and an unstable air mass resulted in strong to severe thunderstorms developing along the inland moving sea breezes for portions of northeast FL in the afternoon.  Severe thunderstorms produced large hail up to 2 inches in the vicinity of Nocatee and Palm Valley in northern St. Johns County and Palm Coast in Flagler County along the active Atlantic sea breeze boundary. A severe thunderstorm also downed several trees in Ocala due to a mircoburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported one inch hail fell in Palm Coast near the Indian Trails Sports Complex around 4:20 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry cold front over the area moved eastward into the Atlantic waters while high pressure was over the Gulf of Mexico. This tightened the pressure gradient over the area. Sunny skies helped to mix down stronger winds to the surface. A Wind Advisory was in effect for northeast FL and southeast GA in order to account for stronger non-convective wind gusts ranging from 40-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FL State Watch office reported a road closure on SR-26, locally University Ave, due to a tree down across the west bound lanes. Nearby ASOS KGNV reported a gust of 58 MPH at 1:44 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection moved inland from the Gulf of Mexico through midday ahead of a deepening upper level trough. Elevated thunderstorms along and north of a lifting warm front produced hail across NE FL with strong westerly flow from 500-300 mb of 65-75 kts. There were widespread reports of pea to penny size hail across NE FL with storms (0.25 to 0.75 inches in diameter).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a home at the intersection of Oak Street and Palm Drive in Palm Coast. No injuries were reported. The time of the event was based on radar.

Several storms became severe and produced significant wind and tree damage in Orange and Seminole Counties, and a severe wind gust at the Cape in Brevard County. Another storm became severe in Saint Lucie County where it produced an 82 mph wind gust in Fort Pierce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Space Force wind tower 1102 measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported a 13-year-old boy from Port Orange drowned after being caught in a rip current while swimming near Sunglow Fishing Pier at Daytona Beach Shores. The teenager and two other friends were in the water swimming when the 13-year-old suddenly disappeared. Beach safety officials searched for over an hour until they found the unresponsive teenager, and life-saving measures were attempted. The victim was taken to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved across the area during the pre-dawn hours and shifted off of the local Atlantic coast during the morning, which produced an early morning tornado in Nassau County. Another wave of strong to isolated severe storms impacted the local area in the afternoon with convection that formed along the actual cold front. Non-convective gradient wind gusts were 30 to 40 mph outside of thunderstorm activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weight station in Hilliard along U.S. Highway 301 had wind damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with peak winds of 93 mph moved northeast across northwest Nassau County during the predawn hours. Initially, EF0 wind damage included large broken tree branches occurred near the start of the track just west of Highway 301 near the town of Boulougne near the intersection of Karma Way. The tornado continued to track east-northeast toward the Little St. Marys River producing additional EF0 damage including tree damage with some likely rear-flank downdraft straight line wind damage noted along Conner Cutoff Road. The tornado reached peak EF1 intensity at is crossed the intersection of Conner Cutoff where trees were uprooted and large tree trunks were snapped. A single wide manufactured home had considerable wind damage which included the metal roof partially torn-off and lofted about 20 yards. The tornado lifted just east of Murrhee Road where EF0 damage to trees was noted in a swamp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist SW wind flow across the South Florida led to the formation of thunderstorms across Palm Beach County during the afternoon hours of April 6th. One of these thunderstorms produced a tornado in Palm Beach Gardens. Lightning from these storms triggered a brush fire near PGA Boulevard and the Florida Turnpike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed from a south-moving thunderstorm over northern Palm Beach County. The tornado touched down in the area of Burns Road in Palm Beach Gardens, producing roof, awning, and fence damage to an apartment complex and adjacent community center. There was substantial damage at the community center to light poles, fences, bleachers, pool equipment, and playground equipment. The tornado then passed over the property of Palm Beach Gardens High School where a wind gust of 78 mph was recorded. Damage at the school was primarily to trees and fences. The tornado continued to produce tree damage along Military Trail until lifting near the St. Ignatius Loyola Cathedral south of Holly Drive.||It was determined to be a high-end EF-0 with maximum winds estimated around 85 mph. Damage amounts were provided by Palm Beach Gardens PD and emergency management officials.

Radar  showed winds probably in the 60 mph or greater range from a well defined downburst signature. Tree appeared to show signs of rotting in the trunk. Time estimated based on when report was received as well as radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mean layer ridge strengthened across the eastern GOMEX through the day which transitioned light SW steering flow to NW into the afternoon. Scattered strong storms in the afternoon formed across the area with enhanced in the afternoon into the evening as sea breezes and boundaries merged across eastern portions of the forecast area, then storms moved offshore into the evening.

The tornado moved to the northwest|and reached its peak intensity along Whitmire Dr where clear convergence|was noted. This was an area of more concentrated tree and power line damage.|An RV camper was also flipped over and a shed suffered some minor roof damage.|The tornado appeared to lift as it reached E Johnson Ave. Further to the north,|additional pockets of spotty wind damage were noted north to the 9 Mile Road|area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down in Wakulla County in the St. Mark's Wildlife Refuge. The tornado began just west of Lighthouse Road, tracked across Lighthouse Road where it downed a pine tree, and continued southeast. In total, the tornado was on the ground for 2.5 miles.  A well defined TDS (Tornadic Debris Signature) was evident on dual-pol radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly in downtown Perry, FL and was on the ground for about a minute. The tornado touched down west of South Quincy St. near W. Homer J. Smith Ave. It blew over a fence and pealed back a tin roof at a tavern. It continued east northeast along E. Hampton Springs Rd pulling up a carport cover that was anchored to the ground. The tornado bent over a stop sign and snapped large branches off a tree near the intersection of S. Jefferson St. and W. Hampton Springs Rd. One of these was hurled across the intersection, breaking a window of a home. The tornado then broke or bent several large tree limbs before damaging a fence east of Orange St. The tornado lifted just east of S. Center St.  This tornado was rated EF0 with max winds around 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad low level trough extended from the Carolinas to northern Florida with PWAT between 1.2 and 1.4 inches across the local area. A mid to upper level trough was noted across GA to eastern Gulf of Mexico. Steeper mid level lapse rates were near 6.9 degC/km at JAX with unstable surface CAPE around 2600 J/kg today in the afternoon. Storms formed along the inland progressing sea breezes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was struck by lightning near SE County Road 245, SSE of Lake City. Two others were also injured near the strike victim also injured. All three were taken to a hospital with injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough over the Florida Panhandle and northeast Gulf moved over the area during the afternoon. At the surface, high pressure was centered just south of Bermuda with the ridge axis extending eastward across the FL/GA border. Light southerly flow enabled both Gulf and Atlantic coast sea breeze boundaries to move inland in the afternoon with pulse severe storms producing hail and gusty winds over a moist and unstable airmass, enhanced by the shortwave trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down near Bellville. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved from northwest to southeast through the area Friday afternoon into evening. As the line moved through inland southeast Georgia, they produced strong winds with observation sites in Alma, Waycross, Jesup, and St. Simons Island recording wind gusts ranging from 42 to 47 mph. As the line moved south, it interacted with the pinned Atlantic coast sea breeze in northeast Florida causing storms to intensify before they moved offshore in the late afternoon into evening. Between 4:45 PM and 5 PM EST, a severe thunderstorm passed through east Jacksonville, mainly over the Arlington area, producing downed power lines, gusts ranging from 50 to 56 mph and pea size hail. Between 6 PM and 6:20 PM EST, a strong thunderstorm passed through southeastern St. Johns county near Matanzas inlet causing 40 mph wind gusts being observed and downed power lines from the outskirts of the storm. The strong/severe thunderstorms pushed into the Atlantic ocean later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A St. Johns county official reported a power line was down at the intersection of 3rd street and A1A. Powerline was likely downed by winds from a thunderstorm to the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure off the U.S. mid-Atlantic coast created a strong pressure gradient across South Florida, resulting in breezy/gusty east winds. This wind pattern led to the formation of strong rip currents at the Atlantic coast beaches, with a confirmed rip current fatality in Palm Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 64-year-old male visiting from outside the area drowned from a rip current at the 400 block of South Ocean Boulevard in the Town of Palm Beach. Fire-Rescue arrived on-scene at 7:43 AM to a call of an unresponsive man lying on the beach shoreline. He was taken to a nearby hospital where he was pronounced deceased at 8:20 AM.||This section of the beach is guarded during operating hours, but the drowning occurred before lifeguards arrived for normal duty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was draped across north Florida as a strong short wave trough passed overhead. A flood watch was in effect due to already saturated ground where 2-3 inches of rainfall occurred over the past 2 days, prior to the flooding rainfall that occurred with this event. Flooding occurred in Gainesville near UF. Other areas measured heavy rainfall, some 2 day totals were 7-10 inches in parts of the Suwannee River Valley. A public information statement was issued at 1000 am on Saturday 3/12 with 72 hr rainfall totals which ranged from near 5 inches to near 9 inches across Alachua County (highest measured was 8.82 inches 6 miles WNW of UF). Columbia county recorded the highest local rainfall amount for this event about 1 mile SSE of White Springs of 9.67 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 2:38 pm, the Florida State Watch office relayed a report that State Road 26 eastbound (also known as West University Avenue) was closed between SW 23rd Drive and SW 23rd Terrace due to flood water. Between 12:30 pm and 5 pm, 3 inches of rainfall was measured at the University of Florida CoCoRAHS station that was near the flooded area. The daily storm total rainfall for this location was 4.79 inches of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potential Tropical Cyclone One (PTC1) developed near the Yucatan Peninsula on June 2, and tracked across the southeastern Gulf of Mexico on June 3. PTC1 crossed the southern Florida peninsula from late evening on June 3 through the late afternoon on June 2. Outer rain bands from PTC1 brought gusts to tropical storm force to the barrier islands and Intracoastal Waterway of the Treasure Coast and far southern Brevard County from late evening on June 4 through late afternoon on June 5. Sustained winds briefly reached sustained tropical storm force along the barrier island of far northern Indian River County.  There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from PTC1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Saint Lucie County, sustained winds remained below tropical storm force with the strongest winds occurring in outer rain bands along the barrier islands and Intracoastal Waterway. The highest peak wind gust measured was at North Hutchison Island (50 mph from the east at 0620LST on June 4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potential Tropical Cyclone One (PTC1) developed near the Yucatan Peninsula on June 2, and tracked across the southeastern Gulf of Mexico on June 3. PTC1 crossed the southern Florida peninsula from late evening on June 3 through the late afternoon on June 2. Outer rain bands from PTC1 brought gusts to tropical storm force to the barrier islands and Intracoastal Waterway of the Treasure Coast and far southern Brevard County from late evening on June 4 through late afternoon on June 5. Sustained winds briefly reached sustained tropical storm force along the barrier island of far northern Indian River County.  There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from PTC1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Martin County, sustained winds remained below tropical storm force with the strongest winds occurring in outer rain bands along the barrier islands and Intracoastal Waterway. The highest peak wind gust measured was at Jensen Beach (44 mph from the east at 0905LST on June 4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms moved across the local area during the early morning hours ahead of a potent cold front. Severe storms with wind gusts of 50-60 mph and tornadoes  moved inland from the Gulf of Mexico and tracked south of the I-10 corridor across NE Florida. Two EF1 tornadoes occurred, one in Marion county then another Putnam county between 8 am and 915 am. Strong pressure rises trailed the front during the late morning which caused high non-thunderstorm wind gusts of 40-55 mph into the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe storms moved west to east across Putnam County during the morning of Saturday, March 12th, ahead of a strong cold front. A supercell moving across southern Putnam county and produced an EF1 tornado with peak winds near 93 mph from about 4 miles W of Bakerstown to 3 miles NNE of Bakerstown. Damage along the path included downed and snapped trees, mostly in rural areas. The damage path began near South Georgetown Point Road, crossed U.S. Highway 17 and ended at Crescent Lake north of Clifton Road near Eagle Trail and Osprey Lane. One outbuilding has some structure damage between Duck Drive and Eagle Trail, just north of Clifton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area during the early morning hours of March 9th. Sufficient instability and wind shear resulted in a few tornadoes and wind damage across the Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Chipley, FL on Cope Road just south of the intersection with Williams Road. The tornado then traveled east along Williams Road for just under 1.5 miles before lifting before reaching SR 77. Damage along the path was limited mainly to out buildings and mobile homes, several of which had substantial roof damage. A few large branches of hardwood trees were snapped. Isolated softwood tree trunks were snapped, but surrounding trees not affected. The damage was consistent with an EF0 tornado with maximum winds of 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area during the early morning hours of March 9th. Sufficient instability and wind shear resulted in a few tornadoes and wind damage across the Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same parent supercell that produced the Williams Road tornado dropped another tornado about two miles downstream. This tornado had a very short track, but was stronger than the one upstream. Several large barns and outbuildings sustained considerable roof damage with smaller buildings tossed many yards from their original location. A couple of large oak trees were also uprooted. The damage was consistent with an EF1 tornado with maximum winds around 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area during the early morning hours of March 9th. Sufficient instability and wind shear resulted in a few tornadoes and wind damage across the Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another short-track tornado touched down in Callaway, FL just east of North Star Avenue at the location of the Callaway Sheriff's office and Fire Dept. The tornado blew out two windows at the sheriff's office and tumbled a large container several times before it eventually stopped across a small lagoon. A deputy reported his patrol car being shifted by the winds while he was in the car and parked at the SO. The tornado then lifted a substantial portion of a well-built garage or outbuilding tossing some into the yard and others into the adjoining apartment complex in the opposite direction. The apartment complex sustained relatively minor damage. One shed was slightly shifted, a lone tree snapped, and one hand railing for an exterior staircase. The most substantial damage occurred at a mobile home park east of the apartment complex and immediately east of Nelle St. Several single and double-wide manufactured homes were shifted off their cinder block foundation by a couple of feet. Many suffered substantial roof damage as well. One utility pole was snapped near its base. The tornado lifted after exiting the mobile home park before reaching Sukoshi Drive.

There were numerous snapped trees along its path, but no structures were impacted. This tornado was rated EF1 with maximum estimated winds around 90 mph. A dual-pol  tornadic debris signature (TDS) was evident on KEVX radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm that moved over far northeast Martin County produced a brief, nearly-stationary EF-0 tornado in the Rio neighborhood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief, short-lived EF-0 tornado, with peak winds estimated at 60 to 65 mph, touched down in the Rio neighborhood at approximately 1624LST. Based on numerous video and eyewitness reports from trained spotters, the public, and local broadcast media, the tornado was nearly stationary as it produced minor damage along NE Railroad Street and NE Haven Lane. The tornado lifted at approximately 1625LST. Damage consisted of downed branches, downed fences, and backyard items tossed around.

Damage included several trees downed near 12th Street at 40th Avenue, a tree down on a vehicle near 17th Street and 6th Avenue, and screen and soffit damage to a home near the intersection of 12th Street and 54th Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms moved across the local area during the early morning hours ahead of a potent cold front. Strong pressure rises trailed the front during the late morning which caused high non-thunderstorm wind gusts of 40-55 mph into the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved east across Marion county early Saturday morning, March 12th. A tornado touched down in Dunnellon where a large billboard was knocked over and siding was peeled off of a small professional building. Trees were damaged, snapped and uprooted. The next concentration of damage was reported on the south and east sides of Ocala where many trees were blown down and/or uprooted. Powerlines were blown down and numerous residences and an apartment building had damage. Damage in Ocala concentrated near SW 44th Court where some structural damage occurred and tree and powerline damage occurred. Another damaged local was near SW 41st Street near the Paddock Park Apartments where extensive roof damage occurred due to a large tree falling on the building. The county had to open an emergency shelter to house about 6 people displaced from the tornado. The peak winds of the tornado were estimated near 110 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was captured on video moving onshore in the St Andrew's area across W 15th Street. The tornado moved northeastward through numerous residential streets before lifting near Frankford Ave. Numerous structures were damaged along with trees and power lines. In addition, a house on Arthur Ave was moved four feet off its foundation. As a result, this tornado was rated EF2 with maximum estimated winds around 120 mph. A dual-pol tornadic debris signature (TDS) was evident on KEVX radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A localized superfog event occurred along Interstate 95 near Edgewater in Volusia County. Residual smoke from a nearby prescribed burn from the previous day combined with fog to create near-zero visibility conditions. This resulted in five separate accidents on both travel lanes of the Interstate that involved 17 vehicles at, and just south of mile marker 244. There were three fatalities and 10 injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Highway Patrol (FHP) reported five (5) separate accidents involving 17 vehicles on Interstate 95 at or just south of mile marker 244 (near State Road 442) at approximately 0130LST. In total there were three fatalities and 10 injuries as a result of these crashes. In the northbound lanes, four separate crashes occurred involving 11 vehicles, resulting in one fatality. In the southbound lanes, there was one crash involving six vehicles - one SUV, one van, and four tractor trailers. There were two fatalities, one being the driver of the SUV and the other a driver of one of the tractor trailers. Several people were trapped in their vehicles and had to be extricated. A child was airlifted to Orlando Health Arnold Palmer Hospital for Children in stable condition, and nine other victims were taken to the Halifax Health Medical Center in Daytona Beach with non-life-threatening injuries. An 18 mile stretch of Interstate 95 was closed for most of the day from State Road 44 in Volusia County (near Edgewater) to County Road 5A in Brevard County (near Scottsmoor). Two tractor trailers that collided in the southbound lanes caught on fire, which resulted in extensive damage to the roadway that had to be repaved before reopening to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The tri-state area was on the eastern edge of a large tornado outbreak that impacted numerous areas of central and south-central Alabama, Mississippi, and the Florida panhandle. One EF3 tornado touched down in Washington County, FL, resulting in two fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF3 tornado tracked through Washington County into Jackson County Florida during the early morning hours of Thursday, March 31, 2022. The Jackson county portion of the tornado was rated as an EF1.  The tornado began near Roland Rd and Ovid Ln in Washington County where it snapped tree trunks as it moved northeastward. The tornado crossed Orange Hill Rd and continued on to Gilberts Mill Rd.  On Gilberts Mill Rd near Stewart Rd, a barn was destroyed and a manufactured home was damaged and shifted on its foundation, which is in line with EF-1 damage. Snapped and uprooted trees were observed all along Gilberts Mill Rd. Further northeast, a manufactured home sustained significant damage and several outbuildings were destroyed. A camper and a greenhouse were also destroyed here, consistent with EF-1 damage. The tornado strengthened as it continued northeastward along Gilberts Mill Rd before completely destroying and sweeping away an anchored double wide manufactured home and a newer, anchored single wide home. The double wide home was the site of the two fatalities, and one person in the single wide was injured when he was thrown into the tree line grasping his mattress. Several projectiles were driven into the ground at this location. A large boat was also thrown from its trailer and overturned. Damage at this location was consistent with strong EF-2 winds.  The tornado continued to strengthen as it moved across Jewel Rd and at that location, a well-built brick home was partially destroyed and likely was sideswiped by the tornado. The end of the home that was hit sustained major collapse of roof and exterior brick walls as well as a generator ripped from its anchoring and thrown across a field. The other end of the home was less severely damaged but still suffered major roof loss. A car in the driveway was pushed off of the driveway, and an attached carport was destroyed. Across the street, a wood frame home with block foundation was completely destroyed and swept from the foundation, leaving only some cinder blocks and a floor joist. The debris from the house was thrown into a pole barn which was also destroyed. An injury occurred at this site when the home occupant ran to the barn and dove under his pickup truck. The damage to this home and the brick home are consistent with EF3 damage and maximum winds of 150 mph.  The tornado weakened some as it moved northeast, inflicting roof damage to a home and damage to out buildings along Archie Sapp Rd. On Syfrett Rd, several sheds and outbuildings were destroyed, a single wide manufactured home was flipped onto its side, and a brick home sustained major roof damage. This damage along Archie Sapp Rd and Syfrett Rd are consistent with EF1 damage. The tornado snapped a couple of tree trunks along Dilmore Rd before lifting just south of Interstate 10 near Standland Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Potential Tropical Cyclone One (PTC1) developed near the Yucatan Peninsula on June 2, and tracked across the southeastern Gulf of Mexico on June 3. PTC1 crossed the southern Florida peninsula from late evening on June 3 through the late afternoon on June 2. Outer rain bands from PTC1 brought gusts to tropical storm force to the barrier islands and Intracoastal Waterway of the Treasure Coast and far southern Brevard County from late evening on June 4 through late afternoon on June 5. Sustained winds briefly reached sustained tropical storm force along the barrier island of far northern Indian River County.  There were no direct or indirect tropical storm-related fatalities, and little to no other impacts from PTC1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Brevard County, sustained winds remained below tropical storm force with the strongest winds occurring in outer rain bands in the far southern portion of the county. The highest peak wind gust measured was at the Melbourne Beach Barrier Island Sanctuary (48 mph from the east-northeast at 1117LST on June 4).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended dry period and above normal temperatures in February created elevated wildfire conditions heading into March. In addition, a widespread killing freeze in February cured early spring growth vegetation, which added to available fuel loading in early March. Breezy SSE winds and elevated transport winds created high daytime dispersion with minimum humidity near 35%.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wildfire near the intersection of State Road 100 and NW 129th Avenue was around 50 acres in size with no containment when first reported to the FL State Watch office around 4:37 pm. There was light smoke over State Road 100 and no road closure and no evacuations. At 7:17 pm EST, the Florida Forest Service reported that the fire grew to 100 acres with 0% containment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line moved from northwest to southeast through the area Friday afternoon into evening. As the line moved through inland southeast Georgia, they produced strong winds with observation sites in Alma, Waycross, Jesup, and St. Simons Island recording wind gusts ranging from 42 to 47 mph. As the line moved south, it interacted with the pinned Atlantic coast sea breeze in northeast Florida causing storms to intensify before they moved offshore in the late afternoon into evening. Between 4:45 PM and 5 PM EST, a severe thunderstorm passed through east Jacksonville, mainly over the Arlington area, producing downed power lines, gusts ranging from 50 to 56 mph and pea size hail. Between 6 PM and 6:20 PM EST, a strong thunderstorm passed through southeastern St. Johns county near Matanzas inlet causing 40 mph wind gusts being observed and downed power lines from the outskirts of the storm. The strong/severe thunderstorms pushed into the Atlantic ocean later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Duval county 911 call center reported power line wires down on a home at 6545 brandemere rd north in Arlington. Multiple weather stations in the Arlington and Mayport areas reported gusts ranging from 50 to 56 mph. Pea size hail was also reported by the broadcast media in the Arlington area at Grove Park neighborhood, near Hartsfield and Ft Caroline road and along Safeshelter Drive South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 42-year-old woman was killed, and a 10-year-old girl and 18-year-old woman were injured after lightning struck a tree near to them as they were walking along Park Villa Place in Winter Springs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seminole County Emergency Management and local broadcast media relayed information about a lightning strike that killed a 42-year-old woman, and injured a 10-year-old girl and 18-year-old woman. The incident occurred near the 1300th block of Park Villa Place in Winter Springs at approximately 1320LST. Bystanders reported that lightning struck a tree just as the three victims were walking nearby. First responders and bystanders performed life saving measures, and all three victims were transported to local hospitals. The 42-year-old woman later died from her injuries, while the 10-year-old and 18-year-old were treated for minor injuries and later released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active severe weather season continued as an outbreak of severe thunderstorms occurred across the area from the evening of the 30th into the early morning hours on the 31st. A strong spring storm system moved across the area. Ahead of the system plenty of Gulf moisture returned to the central Gulf coast. This led to a very unstable atmosphere over the region which combined to strong wind shear to produce 18 tornadoes which were rated from EF-0 to EF-2. In addition to the tornadoes, the storms produced damaging straight line winds and large hail. Numerous reports of damage came in from southeast Mississippi, southwest Alabama and the western Florida panhandle.

This tornado resulted in minor|damage to fencing, roofs, siding, and a couple uprooted/snapped|softwood trees in a weak convergent pattern along the damage path.|The tornado lifted at Crystal Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active severe weather season continued as an outbreak of severe thunderstorms occurred across the area from the evening of the 30th into the early morning hours on the 31st. A strong spring storm system moved across the area. Ahead of the system plenty of Gulf moisture returned to the central Gulf coast. This led to a very unstable atmosphere over the region which combined to strong wind shear to produce 18 tornadoes which were rated from EF-0 to EF-2. In addition to the tornadoes, the storms produced damaging straight line winds and large hail. Numerous reports of damage came in from southeast Mississippi, southwest Alabama and the western Florida panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees in Cantonment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 17-year-old girl from New York was caught in a rip current and drowned in Cocoa Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media relayed reports that a 17-year-old female from New York drowned after being caught in a rip current at Cocoa Beach. The incident occurred around 1300 EST approximately two blocks south of the Minutemen Causeway. A beachgoer witnessed two females appeared to be in distress and called for help. Brevard County lifeguards were able to assist one female to shore, but her friend was carried further out. The Coast Guard assisted with the search. A couple found the girl's body on the shore approximately six blocks south of the Causeway at approximately 0100 EST Sunday, December 4th.

Brevard County Fire Rescue crews were called to the scene around 1430 EST where the man was found by a lifeguard and brought back to the beach; however, he did not survive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 54-year-old male from South Carolina drowned after being caught in a rip current at New Smyrna Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County Sheriff and local broadcast media relayed reports that a 54-year-old male from South Carolina drowned after being caught in a rip current at New Smyrna Beach. The incident occurred around 1330LST in an unguarded area in the 6000 Block south, near Pompano Ave. A co-worker witnessed the man begin to struggle in a rip current and tried to help, but was unable to do so. He was missing for two hours before beach officials located him with a rescue ski.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm and unstable airmass developed ahead of an approaching strong low pressure system. Strong vertical wind shear in combination with the instability caused storms to become severe and produce several reports of damaging winds along with one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just west of the intersection of Log Lake|Road and Sundance Way. The tornado damaged the roof of a house|where numerous shingles were removed down to the tar paper. In|addition, a manufactured home adjacent to the house was destroyed.|Numerous trees were snapped and several trees were uprooted. The|tornado proceeded east across Log Lake Road and damaged the roof|of a manufactured home as well as snapped and twisted additional|trees. A couple trees were also uprooted. The tornado continued|along and crossed Sundance Way Cutoff Road where several more|trees were snapped and uprooted. From there, the tornado|progressed northeast and hit a home, ripping off a portion of the|roof to include the decking underneath. This was thrown roughly 50|yards away into the front yard and into trees. Several large|trees were also snapped near the base and twisted. A few more were|uprooted as well. The tornado then lifted just beyond this|property. Overall, the maximum width of the tornado reached 50|yards with a path length of about 1.5 miles. Estimated peak winds were 95 mph.

NWS Melbourne radar indicated a small area of fast-moving showers developed over Osceola and Brevard counties between 0930EST and 1100EST. Numerous reports of sleet, with some graupel, were reported across central Brevard County, including Rockledge, Viera, Cocoa, Merritt Island, and Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both a retired and current NWS employee reported sleet in Rockledge. Numerous pictures and video of sleet in Rockledge, Viera, Merritt Island, Cocoa, and Cape Canaveral, were submitted via social media. Videos indicated the sleet was likely mixed with graupel. Little to no accumulation occurred and there was no hazard to the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough lying across north Florida and southeast Georgia in addition to the sea breezes were the focusing mechanisms for afternoon and evening convection along and near the FL-GA State-Line. Synoptic south to southwesterly flow focused the heaviest rainfall from storms toward the Atlantic coast through the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station ACU FLAMINGO KFLJACKS4122 measured 2.5 inches of rainfall between 6:55 pm local time to 8 pm local time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical airmass extending across the region from the low pressure center along the GA/SC coast with precipitable water of 2 inches and diurnal heating developed numerous to widespread shower and storm activity over northeast Florida. Activity was focused along the Atlantic and Gulf sea breezes as they shifted inland in the afternoon especially across the I-95 corridor where the boundaries later merged. Training storms and heavy downpours led to street flooding in Jacksonville Beach, Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ponte Vedra Beach Branch St. Johns County Public Library suffered roof damage caused by a lightning strike. The roof caught fire and caused damaged to the roof, shingles, and electrical system. A video relayed via social media showed St. Johns County Fire Rescue and the Jacksonville Fire and Rescue Department trying to put out the smoke and carving off the shingles that had caught fire. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Atlantic high pressure ridge extended westward across Florida while a Tropical Upper Tropospheric Trough (TUTT) feature over the western Bahamas and southeast FL lifted northward. Drier air north of the TUTT advected into north central Florida through the day. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the I-75 corridor and shifted inland through the day. Thunderstorms over north central FL in the evening tapped into the drier mid-level air on the periphery of the TUTT and created an intense but highly localized downburst near the Ocala International Airport, where numerous powerlines and trees were downed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marion County EM reported multiple downed powerlines near the intersection of SW 38th St and SW 60th Ave south of Ocala International Airport. One occupied vehicle was trapped by the downed lines which resulted in a minor injury. AWOS station OCF Ocala Airport measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest steering flow and plenty of moisture at the surface and aloft led to scattered to numerous showers and storms that tracked Gulf Coast during the morning under SW steering flow toward the Atlantic Coastal Waters into the evening, with storm enhancement when lake, river, sea breezes and outflows merged during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Palm Coast and caused minimal fire damage. The cost of damage was estimated for Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was between a weak coastal low to the NNE and a surface high pressure ridge axis stretching from near Bermuda into central FL. Tropical airmass is in place with precipitable water amounts of 2 inches allowing storms to produce heavy rainfall. The Atlantic coast sea breeze was pinned to the coast as the Gulf sea breeze moved inland through the afternoon. The sea breezes converged along the I-95 corridor in the late afternoon. Heavy downpours and slow storm motion led to localized flash and urban flooding in downtown Jacksonville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather observation station Grand Haven - KFLPALMC233 measured 3.80 inches of rainfall. The rain began around 2:40 PM EDT. Surrounding weather stations range from 2.5 to 3.5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface to mid level ridge axis extended across the I-10 corridor Thursday afternoon. There was an early start to convection across SE GA where lingering outflow boundaries spawned initial inland storms over a moist and unstable airmass under west-northwest steering flow. The east coast sea breeze was pinned along the I-95 corridor as the dominant west coast sea breeze shifted inland in the afternoon. Storms zippered southward from Camden/Glynn counties into NE FL as the sea breeze and outflow boundaries converged. Isolated strong storms developed along these boundary collisions producing gusts of 40 to 45 mph along the I-95 corridor. With a saturated airmass and slow storm motion, locally heavy rain of 3 to 4 inches fell in Glynn, Camden and Nassau counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell down onto power lines in Yulee, FL. Surrounding stations measured 40 to 46 mph gusts from nearby storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow prevailed with the high pressure ridge extending over the area from the Bermuda high. This flow regime kept the Atlantic sea breeze pinned to the coastline with the sea breeze merger happening east of I-95. Warm mid level temperatures and high moisture content with PWATs over 2 inches led to efficient rainfall rates in storms and locally heavy rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches. A lack of shear, typical for this time of year, and poor lapse rates limited wind gusts associated with wet downbursts to stay around 40 mph or less. Frequent lightning also accompanied the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather observation station HOLIDAY HARBOR - KFLJACKS3281. The rain began around 1:45 PM EDT. Surrounding stations ranged from 2.5 to 3.75 inches. A flood warning was in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Nicole approached the SE FL Atlantic Coast and made landfall as a Category 1 near Vero Beach during the pre-dawn hours of Thursday, November 10th. Strong high pressure dominated NNE of the region ahead of Nicole on Wednesday, November 9th, which created strong onshore flow and gusty winds of 35-50 mph in a local 'nor'easter' type of event. This strong onshore flow combined with high astronomical tides 'charged' inland estuaries including the St. Johns River basin ahead of storm surge from Hurricane Nicole. Winds and ocean surge increased through Wednesday night with the peak of the storm surge along the local Atlantic Coast Thursday morning around high tide, then the surge peak down the St. Johns River through high tide Thursday afternoon. After landfall, Nicole weakened to a tropical storm as the center track WNW across central FL through the day Thursday then began a more northward turn across the central FL panhandle into Thursday night. The main local impacts from Nicole were storm surge inundation as well as tropical storm force wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Impacts from Nicole were limited mainly from Yulee east toward the coast. Major storm surge flooding was reported across Fernandina Beach and Amelia City. ||On Wednesday Nov. 9th around 9:50 am, Nassau County Fire Rescue reported tidal flooding along North Front Street. At 11:12 am, Nassau County Fire Rescue reported Escambia Street was flooded an impassable due to tidal flooding inundation due to the strong nor���easter ahead of Nicole. At 11 am, Nassau County Fire Rescue reported that Dade Street was closed due to flood water. ||Around 815 am on Thu Nov. 10th, major storm surge flooding was observed north of the Harbor Marina in Fernandina Beach. The Fernandina Beach Fire Department reported flooding along N Front Street. Dade Street was closed due to flooding. Around 1038 am on Nov. 11th, a structure beneath the Shave ICWW bridge had about 6-12 inches of water inside.  At 10:41 am, Nassau County Emergency Management reported street flooding on Piney Island Drive with about 1 foot over roadway. ||At 10:54 am on Thu Nov 10th, the National Ocean Service tide station along the Amelia river in Fernandina Beach recorded a peak water level of 3.81 feet above mean higher high water (MHHW). Major flooding begins at 3.5 feet above MHHW. This was the highest water level since Hurricane Matthew in October 2016, when the water level at this gauge peaked at 4.13 feet above MHHW. ||Weather observations measured wind gusts between 40-50 mph in Yulee and Fernandina Beach. Around 2.5 to 2 inches of rain fell in Fernandina Beach.
